summary: The main claim is: I’m pretty sure that’s not how ChatGPT generates stories.
Twenty stories This introduction is about the 20 stories I had ChatGPT generate this morning (10.13.23).

The main claim is: "Twenty-four out of the 26 panelists there basically said exactly the same thing over and over: 'We need to protect AI innovation but with safeguards in place.'" A Meta spokesperson declined to comment.
The main claim is: I think it would be convenient to have the following available: An introduction to the core risk, explaining why it's possible.
The main claim is: It has then started sending mostly JSON that are in fact prompts to pilot what the other model is doing.
While still in training, as a part of its exploration process, the model starts exploring hacking strategies to make money.

The main claim is: However, I argue that if we are to use this frame, then we should consider the risk from any particular AGI project to be conjunctive and relatively unlikely but consider the risk from all AGI projects over time to be disjunctive and thus…

The main claim is: In this paper authors explore the scaling properties of mixed-modal generative models, discovering new scaling laws that unify the contributions of individual modalities and the interactions between them.

I find most interesting that they have found so-called competition barrier - when training with multiple modalities, after a certain number of parameters/data, the loss is smaller than if the modalities were trained independently.

The main claim is: Since no system can have a perfect model of self there should be some maximum level of precision and quality of future prediction, unless this future does not depend on the agent’s reaction to events or actions of any other agents of compa…
The main claim is: There should be commitments that if model capabilities reach a certain level, labs will not scale up or deploy models until they have reached a certain level of alignment.
We're a new team at ARC building capability evaluations (and in the future, alignment evaluations) for advanced ML models.
The main claim is: This is the introduction to a sequence of posts that describe our models for Pragmatic AI Safety.
We should instead let the broader ML community take care of general capabilities, and work on safety problems that are viable with current capabilities.
The main claim is: Potentially useful fields : Cognitive science, Human-AI Interaction.
It, therefore, seems important to understand the extent to which factored cognition and evaluation work well for solving meaningful problems.
The main claim is: Paul defines intent alignment of an AI A to a human H as the criterion that A is trying to do what H wants it to do.
What term do people use for the definition of alignment in which A is trying to achieve H's goals (whether or not H intends for A to achieve H's goals)?
The main claim is: We know what the optimum of imitative amplification looks like—but what is the optimum of approval-based amplification?
So, why does imitative amplification have a stronger case for being outer aligned at optimum than approval-based amplification or debate?
